Sen. Chuck Schumer (D-N.Y.) is scheduled to discuss an Epstein-related bill on Staten Island on Monday, according to SILive.com.<sup>[1](#ref-1)</sup>

The Senate Majority Leader will address the legislation during his Staten Island appearance, SILive.com reported.<sup>[1](#ref-1)</sup> The announcement comes months after the [Epstein Transparency Act was signed into law](/news/epstein-transparency-act-signed-into-law), mandating that federal agencies declassify and release documents related to Jeffrey Epstein's criminal activities and network of associates.

## Transparency Law Implementation

The [FBI began its review](/news/fbi-begins-epstein-records-review) of Epstein-related records under the new transparency law earlier this year.<sup>[2](#ref-2)</sup> The legislation passed both chambers of Congress with bipartisan support before becoming law.

The statute requires federal agencies to review and release government documents concerning Epstein's crimes, plea agreements, and connections to prominent individuals. Agencies must redact only information that meets specific national security or ongoing investigation criteria.

Schumer has represented New York in the Senate since 1999 and has served as Senate Majority Leader since 2021. Staten Island is one of five boroughs in New York City and falls within Schumer's Senate constituency.

The senator's planned discussion of Epstein-related legislation follows months of debate over document releases and transparency. Federal agencies released documents under court orders and congressional mandate throughout late 2025 and early 2026, revealing previously undisclosed details about Epstein's activities.

## Background on the Case

Epstein died in federal custody in August 2019 while awaiting trial on federal sex trafficking charges. He had been convicted in 2008 on state charges of soliciting a minor under a non-prosecution agreement that drew widespread criticism.

His associate Ghislaine Maxwell was convicted in December 2021 on federal charges of sex trafficking minors. She is serving a 20-year prison sentence.

The Epstein case has generated sustained public and congressional interest due to his documented connections to figures in business, politics, academia, and entertainment. Document releases under the transparency law have exposed communications and relationships that were previously unknown to the public.

Multiple members of Congress have called for complete transparency in the release of Epstein-related files. [Rep. Nancy Mace (R-S.C.)](/news/rep-mace-demands-unredacted-epstein-co-conspirator-files) and [Rep. Thomas Massie (R-Ky.)](/news/massie-rips-trump-doj-over-epstein-file-redactions) have been among the most vocal lawmakers demanding unredacted document releases from the Department of Justice.

Monday's Staten Island event will mark one of the first public appearances by a Senate leader specifically focused on Epstein-related legislation since the transparency law's implementation began.
